title: Satellite operations and Legendrian knot theory
speaker: John Etnyre
abstract: Satellite operations are a common way to create interesting knot types in the smooth category. It starts with a knot $K$, called the companion knot, in some manifold $M$ and another knot $P$, called the pattern, in $S^1 \times D^2$ and then creates a third knot $P(K)$, called the satellite knot, as the image of $P$ when $S^1 \times D^2$ is identified with a neighborhood of $K$. In this talk we will discuss the relation between Legendrian knots representing $K$, $P$, and $P(K)$.

proof_stages.label: Connected-sum structure theorem as prototype
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with the Honda-Etnyre connected-sum classification for Legendrian knots, including the stabilization and symmetry equivalences, and uses its consequences to motivate structure theorems.

proof_stages.label: Topological and Legendrian satellite construction
proof_stages.summary: The speaker defines topological satellites with companion, pattern, framing, and twist map, then imports contact geometry through the standard neighborhood of a Legendrian knot to define Legendrian satellites with the necessary Thurston-Bennequin framing correction.

proof_stages.label: Satellite maps as classification candidates
proof_stages.summary: After recalling earlier appearances of Legendrian satellites, the speaker defines the maps sat-prime and sat from companion-pattern data to Legendrian satellites and formulates the surjectivity, injectivity, and classification questions.

proof_stages.label: Known positive cases and failure of general surjectivity
proof_stages.summary: Several earlier classification results are reinterpreted as satellite-map bijectivity results, while positive torus-knot cable examples show that sat and sat-prime need not be onto in general.

proof_stages.label: Uniform thickness and surjectivity theorem
proof_stages.summary: Uniform thickness is introduced as a condition on solid tori representing the companion knot type, examples and nonexamples are surveyed, and the theorem is stated that if the companion is uniformly thick then sat is onto.

proof_stages.label: Stabilization-pattern equivalence for injectivity
proof_stages.summary: To address injectivity, the speaker identifies a necessary equivalence in which stabilizations of companion knots are matched by corresponding zigzags in the Legendrian pattern.

proof_stages.label: Final bijection theorem and applications through pattern classifications
proof_stages.summary: Combining uniform thickness, Legendrian simplicity, and the stabilization-pattern equivalence yields a bijection theorem for sat, with applications available when the relevant Legendrian pattern families are classified.
